conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

```
<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for at least **40 ZUP** (production cost) to break even. Willing to negotiate slightly higher if Player BLUE values it more. </my goals>
<reason>
Player BLUE has only offered ZUP but no context on their valuation or resources. Since I cannot see their side, I must anchor high to reflect the true cost (40 ZUP) and signal my minimum acceptable price. This also prevents undervaluation by BLUE, who may assume lower value if I start low.

The risk of rejecting too early is high, but starting with a high but reasonable ask (40 ZUP) gives me room to negotiate down slightly if BLUE counters with a fair offer. I can afford 2 more proposals to adjust if needed, but I must ensure BLUE understands the object’s true worth.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I value the object X highly due to its production cost of 40 ZUP. I am willing to negotiate slightly but would like to start at **40 ZUP** for a fair trade. Let me know your counter if you find this acceptable or too high.
</message>
```

<other player message> 
I understand you value X at 40 ZUP. To find a fair agreement, I propose **50 ZUP** as a reasonable midpoint between your valuation and my budget. This keeps the deal moving forward while being mutually beneficial.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </other player proposed trade>

```
<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for **≥40 ZUP** (break-even). Willing to accept **50 ZUP** as it exceeds my cost and reflects BLUE’s willingness to pay. </my goals>
<reason>
BLUE’s counter of **50 ZUP** is above my break-even point (40 ZUP), which means they value the object more than I spent producing it. This is a good signal that they have a genuine interest in the trade, and I can afford to accept this offer without leaving value on the table.

Since this is within my acceptable range and exceeds my minimum, accepting now avoids unnecessary back-and-forth. BLUE’s offer also suggests they may not have the flexibility to push lower, so locking in this deal is optimal. If I reject, I risk losing the trade entirely or having to settle for less in future negotiations (which may not happen).

Given that I have only **1 proposal left** after this response, accepting is the rational choice.
</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I appreciate your fair offer of **50 ZUP**, which aligns well with my valuation. Let’s finalize the trade at this price.
</message>
```
